content.text: Post-launch, a metric improves. Did the GenAI system cause it? Possibly — but the same period may have included a process redesign, new agent training, a product change that deflected easier queries, or seasonal patterns. Attribution is the discipline of decomposing the observed delta into its contributing causes and assigning each one a credible share.
Attribution is not about claiming credit. It is about understanding causation so that future investment decisions are made on accurate evidence rather than correlation.

GenAI programmes are particularly exposed to attribution errors because: (1) deployments often coincide with process changes; (2) the model's influence is indirect and distributed across thousands of micro-interactions; and (3) sponsors are motivated to claim large benefits, while sceptics are motivated to discount them. A structured attribution model is the only way to produce a number that both parties can accept.

attrs.alt: Post-launch benefits tracking showing AHT and FCR trends over 16 weeks with a confound annotation at W+8 and an attribution breakdown pie showing 65% GenAI, 26% process redesign, 9% training
attrs.title: Post-Launch Benefits Tracking Attribution Model

content.text: The diagram shows AHT (average handle time) improving from 8.4 min to 6.1 min over 16 weeks. A product change at W+8 is logged as a confound. The attribution breakdown at W+16 assigns 65% of the improvement (1.5 min) to the GenAI suggestion engine, 26% (0.6 min) to process redesign, and 9% (0.2 min) to new agent training. Each share is derived from controlled analysis rather than opinion.
Benefits do not accrue linearly. The first weeks after launch typically show a
as users adjust and adoption ramps; steady-state benefits emerge 6–12 weeks post-launch. Measurement must continue long enough to capture the full adoption curve.
Adoption rate, early signal, operational issues
Three attribution methods are commonly used for GenAI programmes, ordered from most to least rigorous:

: randomly assign a portion of users or work items to the GenAI-assisted group and a control group. The difference in metrics between groups is cleanly attributable to the GenAI system. This is the gold standard but requires sufficient volume (typically 1,000+ events per group) and a system that can gate access per user.

: compare the before/after change in a GenAI-using group against the before/after change in a comparable non-using group (different team, region, or product line). The GenAI effect is estimated as: (treated post − treated pre) − (control post − control pre). Requires a plausible control group.

: when A/B testing and DiD are not feasible, convene a working group of the PM, business owner, process owner, and a neutral analyst to estimate the share of the observed delta attributable to each intervention (GenAI, process change, training, etc.). Shares must sum to 100% and be documented with supporting rationale. Less rigorous but more tractable for operational programmes.
It is rare for GenAI to be the sole driver of a benefit. Partial attribution — where multiple interventions each receive a share — must be documented clearly and must not be inflated. Common errors:
Claiming 100% attribution to GenAI when a concurrent process change also contributed.
Summing overlapping attribution across multiple reports to double-count the same delta.
Attributing seasonal tailwinds (e.g., lower ticket volume in summer) to GenAI improvements.
Reporting gross savings without deducting the ongoing cost of running the GenAI system (inference costs, maintenance, support).

The benefits realisation report is a quarterly document that compares actuals to the locked baseline for every metric in the benefits register. It is separate from the weekly programme status report — it addresses a different question: 'Are the promised benefits materialising?' rather than 'Is the project on schedule?'

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class BenefitRealisationReport:
    programme: str
    period: str                         # e.g. "Q2 2025"
    report_date: date
    metrics: list[BenefitActual]
    attribution_method: str             # "A/B test", "DiD", or "Expert decomposition"
    net_benefit_calc: Optional[NetBenefitCalculation]
    narrative: str                      # 2-3 sentence plain-language summary
    next_review_date: date

    def overall_rag(self) -> str:
        rags = [m.rag() for m in self.metrics]
        if "RED" in rags:
            return "RED"
        if "AMBER" in rags:
            return "AMBER"
        return "GREEN"

    def metrics_on_track(self) -> list[BenefitActual]:
        return [m for m in self.metrics if m.rag() == "GREEN"]

    def metrics_at_risk(self) -> list[BenefitActual]:
        return [m for m in self.metrics if m.rag() in ("AMBER", "RED")]

    def formatted_summary(self) -> str:
        on_track = len(self.metrics_on_track())
        at_risk = len(self.metrics_at_risk())
        net = ""
        if self.net_benefit_calc:
            net = f" | Net annual benefit: £{self.net_benefit_calc.net_annual_benefit:,.0f}"
        return (
            f"[{self.period}] {self.programme} — Benefits: {self.overall_rag()}  "
            f"{on_track} on track / {at_risk} at risk{net}"
        )


def generate_quarterly_report(
    register: BenefitsRegister,
    period: str,
    attribution_method: str,
    net_calc: Optional[NetBenefitCalculation],
    narrative: str,
    next_review: date,
) -> BenefitRealisationReport:
    """Collect the latest actual for each metric and produce the quarterly report."""
    metric_ids = {a.metric_id for a in register.actuals}
    latest = [register.latest_for(mid) for mid in metric_ids if register.latest_for(mid)]
    return BenefitRealisationReport(
        programme=register.programme,
        period=period,
        report_date=date.today(),
        metrics=latest,
        attribution_method=attribution_method,
        net_benefit_calc=net_calc,
        narrative=narrative,
        next_review_date=next_review,
    )

Distribute the quarterly benefits report to the same steering audience that approved the business case. Benefits that are GREEN for three consecutive quarters can be de-risked from the benefits register and recognised in financial reporting. Benefits that are RED for two consecutive quarters must be reviewed: either the target was unrealistic (amend the baseline formally) or the GenAI system is underperforming (investigate and remediate).
The locked pre-launch baseline should not be revised casually. Three legitimate reasons to formally re-baseline:

: a regulatory change, a market shock, or a product discontinuation fundamentally alters the operating environment such that the original baseline is no longer a valid comparator. Document the trigger, the new baseline value, and who authorised the change.
: the GenAI system is extended to additional use cases or user populations not in the original scope. The baseline for the new scope must be measured before the expansion launches.

: an error in the original data extraction is discovered post-launch. If the error materially changes the baseline (>5% impact), a formal correction is required with sign-off from the original validators.
to document co-occurring changes, and a

to isolate the GenAI contribution. A/B testing is the most rigorous approach; expert decomposition is acceptable when randomised experiments are infeasible. Net benefit calculations must deduct ongoing GenAI operating costs to avoid overstating ROI. All attribution statements should be co-signed by the PM and business owner to prevent post-hoc revision.
